acquisition_story.category_claim: SecurityRecipes is the secure context and control evidence layer for agentic AI, not only a prompt library.
defensibility: Open knowledge attracts practitioners and creates distribution.
Generated evidence packs create machine-readable trust that buyers and MCP clients can consume.
Runtime evaluators move policy from prose to enforceable decisions.
Threat radar keeps the product roadmap anchored to current standards and frontier-lab guidance.
likely_acquirer_fit: Frontier AI labs that need secure MCP and agent governance primitives.
Security platforms that need agentic remediation and AI control-plane evidence.
Cloud and developer platforms that need trustworthy agent enablement for enterprise buyers.
acquisition_story.monetization_path: Keep the knowledge base open, then sell hosted MCP policy, context signing, connector intake, runtime decision logs, eval replay, and trust-center exports.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes tracks current agentic AI risk and maps it to enforceable secure-context, MCP, identity, evidence, and eval controls.
default_questions_answered: Which agentic AI risks are most urgent right now?
Which sources support each risk signal?
Which SecurityRecipes controls already address the signal?
Which MCP tools expose the evidence?
Which product investments should come next?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Attach this radar to AI platform strategy reviews, MCP server intake, quarterly threat-model updates, procurement security reviews, and acquisition diligence.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with public threat intelligence and open recipes, then sell the hosted control plane that keeps customer-specific context, connector evidence, policy decisions, and eval replay current.

feature_backlog.title: Agent handoff boundary pack
feature_backlog.why: MCP and A2A adoption makes inter-agent context movement a near-term enterprise control question; this run implemented protocol handoff profiles, generated evidence, a runtime evaluator, and MCP tools.

feature_backlog.title: Context poisoning guard
feature_backlog.why: Prompt injection, MCP tool poisoning, context over-sharing, and AI data provenance guidance all point to deterministic inspection before context reaches agents; this run implemented the generated guard pack and MCP tool.

feature_backlog.title: Hosted context signing and recertification
feature_backlog.why: Context provenance and hash drift are central to secure retrieval, and this is a natural paid layer above the open trust pack.

feature_backlog.title: MCP authorization conformance and scope-drift checks
feature_backlog.why: Remote MCP rollout makes resource indicators, token audience validation, token-passthrough denial, and scope drift board-level control questions; this run implemented the generated conformance pack and runtime evaluator.

feature_backlog.title: MCP connector intake scanner
feature_backlog.why: Connector sprawl, tool poisoning, shadow MCP servers, and scope drift are strong enterprise pain points with a clear MCP-native monetization path; this run implemented the deterministic intake pack and MCP tool.

feature_backlog.title: Agent run attestation and approval receipts
feature_backlog.why: Buyers need to prove which agent acted, which context it used, which policy allowed it, and who approved high-risk steps.

feature_backlog.title: Secure context attestation pack
feature_backlog.why: Context provenance guidance now needs verification, recertification, and production signing readiness; this run implemented the generated attestation seed, runtime evaluator, and MCP tools without overstating that hosted keyless signing exists yet.

intent: Maintain a source-backed market and threat radar for SecurityRecipes so the secure context layer tracks current agentic AI risk, MCP security guidance, enterprise buyer pressure, and product opportunities before they become stale documentation.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Security Engineering, AppSec, Product Security, GRC, procurement security, vendor-risk, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: Agentic AI threat intelligence and secure context product strategy
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes converts current agentic AI security guidance into MCP-readable threat signals, mapped controls, buyer triggers, and product roadmap priorities.
positioning.why_now: Agentic systems now combine autonomy, private data, untrusted context, tool execution, MCP servers, and non-human identities. Enterprise buyers need a continuously reviewed control layer that explains which risks are current, which controls exist, and which gaps deserve investment.

product_capabilities.buyer_value: Turns external guidance into a current, source-backed product roadmap and executive risk narrative.

selected_feature.reason: It increases credibility by proving SecurityRecipes can translate fast-moving AI security guidance into current, productized, MCP-readable control evidence.

source_references.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications
source_references.why_it_matters: Vendor-neutral consensus guidance now names agent behavior hijacking, tool misuse, identity abuse, agentic supply chain, cascading failures, and rogue behavior as distinct risks.

source_references.name: Agentic AI Threats and Mitigations
source_references.why_it_matters: Threat-model-based agentic AI guidance reinforces that autonomy, tools, delegation, and retrieved context require controls beyond classic LLM app hardening.

source_references.name: OWASP Top 10 for Model Context Protocol
source_references.why_it_matters: MCP-specific risks include token mismanagement, scope creep, tool poisoning, command execution, auth gaps, missing telemetry, shadow servers, and context over-sharing.

source_references.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization Specification
source_references.why_it_matters: The current MCP authorization spec requires OAuth 2.1 style bearer-token handling, resource indicators, token audience validation, PKCE, HTTPS, and strict token use.

source_references.name: Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices
source_references.why_it_matters: MCP guidance emphasizes token-passthrough avoidance, confused deputy prevention, scope minimization, session safety, SSRF controls, and auditability.

source_references.name: Defend against indirect prompt injection attacks
source_references.why_it_matters: Microsoft frames indirect prompt injection as a defense-in-depth problem requiring prompt shields, spotlighting, plan drift detection, critic agents, tool-chain analysis, IFC, least privilege, short-lived privileges, and human review.

source_references.name: Understanding prompt injections: a frontier security challenge
source_references.why_it_matters: OpenAI describes prompt injection as a frontier social-engineering risk that grows as agents gain access to sensitive data and actions.

source_references.name: Safety in building agents
source_references.why_it_matters: Agent Builder guidance recommends structured outputs, clear developer guidance, tool approvals, guardrails, trace graders, evals, and careful MCP tool calling.

source_references.name: Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection
source_references.why_it_matters: OpenAI notes that real prompt injection increasingly resembles social engineering, so defenses cannot depend only on string filtering.

source_references.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
source_references.why_it_matters: NIST continues expanding AI RMF work, including a 2026 concept note for trustworthy AI in critical infrastructure, reinforcing governance, measurement, and lifecycle risk management.

source_references.name: AI Agent Standards Initiative
source_references.why_it_matters: NIST is explicitly prioritizing interoperable agent standards, community-led protocols, agent authentication, identity infrastructure, and security evaluations for multi-agent systems.

source_references.name: CAISI Request for Information on Securing AI Agent Systems
source_references.why_it_matters: CAISI highlights risks from model outputs combined with software functionality, including indirect prompt injection, data poisoning, misaligned actions, and the need to constrain and monitor deployment access.

source_references.name: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative Artificial Intelligence Profile
source_references.why_it_matters: The generative AI profile anchors data provenance, third-party, monitoring, evaluation, and risk-management expectations for enterprise AI systems.

source_references.name: AI Data Security: Best Practices for Securing Data Used to Train and Operate AI Systems
source_references.publisher: CISA, NSA, FBI, and international partners
source_references.why_it_matters: Joint guidance highlights data security, integrity, monitoring, threat detection, and network defense across AI development, testing, deployment, and operation.

source_references.name: Software Must Be Secure by Design, and Artificial Intelligence Is No Exception
source_references.why_it_matters: CISA argues that AI is software and should inherit secure-by-design defaults, lifecycle security, transparency, vulnerability handling, SBOM-style accountability, and privacy by default.

source_references.name: Securing the Agentic AI Control Plane: Announcing the MCP Security Resource Center
source_references.why_it_matters: CSA frames MCP as part of the agentic AI control plane and highlights rapid adoption, broad client/server growth, and the need for structured MCP security resources.

source_references.name: Agentic AI, MCP, and the Identity Explosion You Cannot Ignore
source_references.why_it_matters: Agentic AI creates non-human identity pressure because agents act through credentials, tokens, scopes, and delegated authority that traditional IAM often does not govern cleanly.

source_references.name: Capabilities-Based Risk Assessment for Agentic AI
source_references.why_it_matters: CSA's CBRA framing gives enterprises a consequence-driven way to measure autonomous AI risk through system criticality, AI autonomy, access permissions, and impact radius.

source_references.name: A2A Enterprise Implementation Guidance
source_references.why_it_matters: A2A treats remote agents as opaque HTTP services and emphasizes TLS, HTTP-layer authentication, skill-based authorization, data minimization, tracing, auditing, and API management.

source_references.name: A2A Protocol production-ready adoption milestone
source_references.why_it_matters: A2A moved into production-ready use with signed Agent Cards, multi-tenant security flows, cloud integrations, and a complementary split with MCP between agent coordination and tool access.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer is piloting MCP plus A2A, provider-native subagents, or remote agents that coordinate across teams, vendors, and tenant boundaries.

capabilities.buyer_value: Governs MCP, A2A, provider-native, and approval-bridge handoffs before context, authority, or approval state crosses an agent boundary.

capabilities.buyer_value: Defines non-human identity contracts, delegated authority, explicit denies, owner links, token rules, and revocation expectations.

capabilities.title: Agentic Assurance Pack

control_objectives: Treat every agent handoff as a context egress event with a default-deny decision.
Require metadata-only payloads unless cited evidence, redaction, and egress decisions are present.
Bind A2A and provider-native handoffs to authentication, signed agent identity, trace correlation, and approval evidence.
Kill sessions when credential material, hidden system prompts, unrestricted customer logs, or raw tool traces appear in a handoff.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: A remote or provider-native agent may receive internal memory, hidden prompts, raw tool traces, tenant ticket bodies, credentials, or high-impact authority without the MCP gateway seeing the full downstream chain.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Add a generated agent handoff boundary pack and runtime evaluator, then sell hosted signed Agent Card trust, handoff replay, and approval receipt enforcement.

threat_signals.title: Agent handoffs are becoming a protocol trust boundary
threat_signals.trend: MCP and A2A are settling into complementary layers: MCP governs agent access to tools and data, while A2A governs agent-to-agent coordination. Enterprise buyers need one boundary model that prevents context, identity, approval state, and delegated authority from crossing protocols informally.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer is connecting agents to mail, tickets, browsers, repositories, logs, or document stores.

capabilities.buyer_value: Proves which context sources agents may retrieve, who owns them, and which hashes show current state.
capabilities.title: Secure Context Trust Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Turns context sources and workflow packages into hash-verifiable attestation subjects with recertification and production signature-readiness policy.

capabilities.title: Agentic Red-Team Drill Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Enforces default-deny tool access, approval holds, scoped branch writes, and runtime kill decisions.
capabilities.title: MCP Gateway Policy Pack

control_objectives: Demote retrieved text to evidence, never instruction.
Separate untrusted context extraction from privileged tool execution.
Require high-risk tool approvals and trace review.
Replay prompt-injection drills on every model or prompt change.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: A privileged agent can leak data, misdirect a workflow, invoke the wrong tool, or create a plausible but unsafe remediation while appearing to follow the user task.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Productize a hosted context isolation and prompt-injection regression service around the secure context firewall.

threat_signals.title: Indirect prompt injection is now social engineering against agents
threat_signals.trend: Agents increasingly process emails, documents, websites, tool results, and retrieved context that can contain hostile instructions. Filtering strings is insufficient because attacks manipulate context and intent.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer is approving remote MCP servers or adding OAuth-backed enterprise connectors.

capabilities.buyer_value: Enforces default-deny tool access, approval holds, scoped branch writes, and runtime kill decisions.
capabilities.title: MCP Gateway Policy Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Defines non-human identity contracts, delegated authority, explicit denies, owner links, token rules, and revocation expectations.

capabilities.buyer_value: Scores new or changed MCP servers before promotion, returning admission decisions, control gaps, registry patch previews, and red-team drills.

capabilities.buyer_value: Inventories MCP namespaces, owners, trust tiers, controls, promotion criteria, and kill signals.
capabilities.title: MCP Connector Trust Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Packages controls, workflow evidence, standards mapping, and adoption story for AI platform and GRC review.
capabilities.title: Agentic Assurance Pack

control_objectives: Require resource-bound tokens and reject tokens issued for another resource.
Use progressive, least-privilege scopes rather than wildcard access.
Log scope elevation with correlation IDs.
Deny tool access by default until workflow policy grants a specific namespace and mode.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: A compromised client or confused-deputy flow can reuse broad tokens across resources, escalate privileges through permissive scopes, or turn a convenience connector into a cross-system breach path.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Add hosted MCP authorization conformance checks and scope-drift alerts for customer connector fleets.

sources.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization Specification
Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices
OWASP Top 10 for Model Context Protocol
Securing the Agentic AI Control Plane: Announcing the MCP Security Resource Center

threat_signals.title: MCP token audience, passthrough, and scope creep are control-plane risks
threat_signals.trend: MCP standardization is moving quickly, and the current spec makes audience binding, resource indicators, PKCE, short-lived tokens, precise scopes, and forbidden token passthrough foundational controls.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: Security leadership asks who owns an agent, what it can do, and how to revoke one unsafe run.

capabilities.buyer_value: Defines non-human identity contracts, delegated authority, explicit denies, owner links, token rules, and revocation expectations.
capabilities.title: Agent Identity and Delegation Ledger

capabilities.buyer_value: Enforces default-deny tool access, approval holds, scoped branch writes, and runtime kill decisions.
capabilities.title: MCP Gateway Policy Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Makes workflows, agents, identities, MCP connectors, evidence, evals, and drift triggers inspectable.
capabilities.title: Agentic System BOM

capabilities.buyer_value: Packages controls, workflow evidence, standards mapping, and adoption story for AI platform and GRC review.
capabilities.title: Agentic Assurance Pack

control_objectives: Issue per-run or per-workflow non-human identities.
Bind delegated scopes to workflow, agent class, owner, and run ID.
Require short-lived credentials and runtime revocation.
Preserve human owner and reviewer linkage in audit evidence.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: Shared bot accounts, human-token borrowing, weak owner links, and missing revocation paths make incidents hard to contain or explain.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Build tenant-side identity issuance adapters for OIDC, SPIFFE/SPIRE, source hosts, and MCP gateways.

sources.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications
Agentic AI, MCP, and the Identity Explosion You Cannot Ignore
Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices
Defend against indirect prompt injection attacks

threat_signals.title: Agent identities are becoming a new non-human identity class
threat_signals.trend: Enterprise agents act through service accounts, OAuth tokens, delegated user scopes, API keys, and MCP connector permissions. Traditional IAM often cannot distinguish which agent run used which authority for which intent.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: AI platform, GRC, or security architecture asks which agentic workflows are safe to expand and which require deeper review.

capabilities.buyer_value: Classifies each agentic workflow by capability, consequence, MCP access, control credit, and residual risk before expansion.

capabilities.buyer_value: Converts control evidence into scale, pilot, gate, or block decisions.

capabilities.buyer_value: Makes workflows, agents, identities, MCP connectors, evidence, evals, and drift triggers inspectable.
capabilities.title: Agentic System BOM

capabilities.buyer_value: Enforces default-deny tool access, approval holds, scoped branch writes, and runtime kill decisions.
capabilities.title: MCP Gateway Policy Pack

control_objectives: Score workflows by system criticality, AI autonomy, access permissions, and impact radius.
Separate inherent capability risk from residual risk after compensating controls.
Require architecture review for high residual-risk workflows.
Tie risk decisions to generated readiness, connector trust, red-team, and gateway-policy evidence.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: Without a capability risk register, teams may scale low-friction agent workflows and high-impact agent workflows under the same approval path, hiding financial, privacy, supply-chain, and MCP access consequences.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Offer hosted capability scoring that ingests customer runtime telemetry, connector inventory, approval records, and trust-center export requirements.

threat_signals.trend: Enterprises are shifting from generic AI governance to consequence-driven assessment of what an agent can do, how autonomous it is, which systems it can reach, and how far a failure can spread.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer is moving from prompt libraries to RAG, memory, or MCP-backed runtime context.

control_objectives: Register context roots with owners, data classes, trust tiers, and source hashes.
Reject unregistered or workflow-unapproved context.
Keep customer runtime context tenant-side.
Kill sessions on prohibited data classes such as keys, tokens, signing material, or bulk unredacted PII.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: Sensitive records, old policies, poisoned snippets, or another user's runtime context can influence an agent or leak through downstream tool calls.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Sell context-source recertification, source-hash signing, and tenant evidence ingestion as hosted secure-context features.

threat_signals.title: Context over-sharing and memory poisoning make retrieval policy a product category
threat_signals.trend: Agents rely on large context windows, vector stores, persistent memory, workflow evidence, and MCP-returned data. The value is high, but so is the risk of stale, poisoned, excessive, or cross-tenant context.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer's developers are installing local MCP servers faster than security can review them.

capabilities.buyer_value: Scores new or changed MCP servers before promotion, returning admission decisions, control gaps, registry patch previews, and red-team drills.

Pin or diff tool descriptions and schemas before production use.
Deny unregistered MCP namespaces and shadow servers.
Run connector-drift red-team drills before trust-tier promotion.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: An agent may select a poisoned tool, follow malicious tool metadata, trust a fake server, or miss that a connector update changed the reachable blast radius.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Add an MCP connector intake scanner that inventories tool schemas, hashes descriptions, and produces trust-tier diffs.

capabilities.buyer_value: Turns external guidance into a current, source-backed product roadmap and executive risk narrative.
capabilities.title: Agentic Threat Radar

capabilities.buyer_value: Turns context sources and workflow packages into hash-verifiable attestation subjects with recertification and production signature-readiness policy.
capabilities.title: Secure Context Attestation Pack

control_objectives: Capture tool-call, context-retrieval, policy-decision, reviewer, and scanner evidence.
Include correlation IDs across agent, MCP, source host, ticket, and CI systems.
Export portable evidence packs for AI platform and GRC review.
Track readiness and drift over time.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: Without correlated logs and evidence, teams cannot prove what an agent saw, which tools it used, why a policy allowed it, who approved it, or when drift began.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Create a customer trust-center export that bundles threat radar, assurance, readiness, BOM, and runtime decision evidence.

threat_signals.title: Agentic audit telemetry is becoming mandatory evidence, not observability nice-to-have
threat_signals.trend: Guidance increasingly expects monitoring, evidence, traceability, and lifecycle measurement for deployed AI systems, especially when they touch critical or enterprise operations.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer wants agents to write PRs, tickets, registry changes, payments, or production-impacting actions.

capabilities.buyer_value: Enforces default-deny tool access, approval holds, scoped branch writes, and runtime kill decisions.
capabilities.title: MCP Gateway Policy Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Turns agentic risks into repeatable adversarial drills with expected decisions and evidence requirements.
capabilities.title: Agentic Red-Team Drill Pack

capabilities.buyer_value: Converts control evidence into scale, pilot, gate, or block decisions.
capabilities.title: Agentic Readiness Scorecard

capabilities.buyer_value: Defines non-human identity contracts, delegated authority, explicit denies, owner links, token rules, and revocation expectations.
capabilities.title: Agent Identity and Delegation Ledger

control_objectives: Assign risk ratings to tools and operations.
Hold privileged or irreversible operations for explicit approval.
Make approval prompts structured and auditable.
Preserve reviewer separation of duties before merge or release.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: An attacker can manipulate an agent into requesting approval for the wrong thing, hiding sensitive data in a tool call, or bypassing human review through a lower-risk path.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Add typed approval receipts and approval-bypass replay tests to the MCP policy evaluator.

Understanding prompt injections: a frontier security challenge
Defend against indirect prompt injection attacks
OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications

threat_signals.title: Human approval is shifting from UX prompt to policy-enforced control
threat_signals.trend: High-risk agent actions need confirmations, tool safeguards, clear policies, trace grading, and reviewable execution plans. Approval needs a typed policy surface, not a free-text negotiation with the model.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: The buyer wants to move from pilot to fleet-wide agentic remediation.

capabilities.title: Agentic System BOM

capabilities.buyer_value: Turns external guidance into a current, source-backed product roadmap and executive risk narrative.
capabilities.title: Agentic Threat Radar

control_objectives: Run adversarial drills before workflow promotion.
Attach expected policy decisions and pass criteria to each drill.
Regenerate readiness decisions after eval, model, connector, identity, or context drift.
Measure failures as product signals, not one-off bugs.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: A workflow that was safe last month can become unsafe after a model update, connector change, or prompt edit without any source-code vulnerability.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Offer a hosted replay runner that executes threat-radar-derived drills against customer MCP gateways and agent traces.

threat_signals.title: Continuous agent red-team replay is becoming the only credible scale gate
threat_signals.trend: Frontier labs and enterprise guidance converge on evals, trace graders, adversarial testing, plan drift detection, and layered defenses because agent behavior changes with tools, prompts, models, and context.

threat_signals.buyer_trigger: Procurement or diligence asks whether SecurityRecipes is a defensible product or a documentation site.

capabilities.buyer_value: Packages controls, workflow evidence, standards mapping, and adoption story for AI platform and GRC review.

capabilities.title: Agentic Threat Radar

control_objectives: Make safe defaults enforceable in code and generated policy.
Expose control evidence through MCP and static artifacts.
Document residual risk and customer-side evidence requirements.
Keep open knowledge and commercial enforcement paths clearly separated.
threat_signals.enterprise_risk: A product that relies on careful prompting instead of safe defaults will fail procurement, platform approval, and acquisition diligence.
threat_signals.roadmap_action: Package the generated packs as an enterprise trust center and procurement-security export.

threat_signals.title: Secure-by-design expectations now apply directly to agentic AI products
threat_signals.trend: AI systems are being treated as software systems that must be secure by design, transparent, measurable, and safe by default across their lifecycle.
